title: Gururatnatrayastotra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: 1st-2nd millennium CE (undatable precisely; transmitted in Nepalese manuscripts)
summary: A devotional hymn praising the Guru together with the Three Jewels (ratnatraya): the Buddha, the Dharma, and the Sangha. It typically extols the qualities of the spiritual teacher and the three refuges that form the foundation of Buddhist practice, often combining sub-hymns to each Jewel (buddharatna-, dharmaratna-, sangharatna-stotra).
history: An anonymous Buddhist Sanskrit stotra preserved in the manuscript traditions of the Kathmandu Valley and digitized by the Digital Sanskrit Buddhist Canon (DSBC) project and Sanskrit Documents. Such guru- and ratnatraya-stotras reflect the centrality of guru-devotion and refuge in Vajrayana liturgy; precise authorship and date are not established.

title: Caṇḍikādaṇḍakastotra
author: anonymous/traditional
tradition: Buddhist (Vajrayana; Newar Buddhism), with the goddess shared with Hindu Shakta imagery
summary: A hymn in the long, galloping daṇḍaka metre addressed to the fierce goddess Caṇḍikā. It praises her terrifying and protective aspects, invoking her power to destroy obstacles and demons. The daṇḍaka form, with its very long lines, lends the praise a torrential, incantatory quality suited to a wrathful deity.
history: A Sanskrit stotra transmitted in Buddhist (and overlapping Hindu Shakta) circles; the Buddhist recension was transliterated for the Digital Sanskrit Buddhist Canon. Caṇḍikā/Caṇḍī is a fierce form of the Great Goddess venerated across traditions, and daṇḍaka-stotras to her circulated widely; authorship of this piece is anonymous.

title: Mahogratārāṣṭakastotra
author: anonymous/traditional
summary: An eight-verse hymn (aṣṭaka) in praise of Mahogratārā ('Great Fierce Tārā'), a wrathful, dark-blue form of the savioress goddess Tārā closely associated with Ekajaṭā/Ugratārā. It extols her power to rescue devotees from fear, danger, and obstacles.
history: An anonymous Vajrayana stotra to a fierce Tārā form, preserved in Nepalese Buddhist manuscript collections and digitized by the DSBC. Ugra/Mahogra Tārā is a prominent wrathful deity in Buddhist Tantra; many short stotras and aṣṭakas to her circulated. It should not be confused with Sarvajñamitra's famous Sragdharā-stotra to Ārya-Tārā.

title: Mahogratārāstuti
author: anonymous/traditional
summary: A hymn of praise (stuti) to Mahogratārā, the 'Great Fierce Tārā,' a wrathful blue form of the goddess Tārā. It glorifies her terrifying iconography and her saving, obstacle-removing power, functioning as a companion devotional piece to the corresponding aṣṭaka.
history: An anonymous Buddhist Tantric praise of Ugra/Mahogra Tārā preserved in Nepalese manuscripts and digitized in the DSBC corpus. It belongs to the cluster of short Tārā stotras current in Newar Vajrayana liturgy; author and date are unrecorded.

title: Dharmadhātuvāgīśvaramaṇḍalastotra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: 1st-2nd millennium CE (undatable precisely; deity form known from the Sādhanamālā era, c. 5th-11th c.)
summary: A hymn praising the maṇḍala of Dharmadhātuvāgīśvara, an esoteric, multi-armed and multi-faced form of the bodhisattva Mañjuśrī ('Lord of Speech of the Dharma-realm'). The text venerates the central deity and the assembly of the maṇḍala, a major cultic focus in Newar Buddhism of the Kathmandu Valley.
history: Dharmadhātuvāgīśvara is a form of Mañjuśrī whose sādhana is described in the Sādhanamālā; his maṇḍala remained an important object of worship and art in Nepal. This anonymous maṇḍala-stotra is preserved in Nepalese manuscripts and digitized in the DSBC/Sanskrit Documents corpus.

title: Vāgīśvaravarṇanāstotra
author: anonymous/traditional
summary: A descriptive hymn (varṇanā-stotra) praising Vāgīśvara, the 'Lord of Speech,' i.e. the bodhisattva Mañjuśrī in his form as patron of eloquence and learning. It describes his iconography and qualities and celebrates him as the tutelary deity revered throughout Nepal.
history: Vāgīśvara-Mañjuśrī is the tutelary deity of Nepalese Buddhists, his mantra 'oṃ vāgīśvara mūḥ' inscribed widely in the Kathmandu Valley. This anonymous praise-poem is preserved in the Nepalese Buddhist manuscript tradition and digitized in the DSBC corpus.

title: Vajrapāṇināmāṣṭottaraśatastotra
author: anonymous/traditional
summary: A litany-hymn enumerating the 108 names (aṣṭottaraśata-nāma) of Vajrapāṇi, the bodhisattva who holds the vajra and embodies the Buddha's power and protective energy. Each name extols an aspect of the deity, and recitation of the full set functions as both praise and protective invocation.
history: Lists of 108 names form a standard devotional genre across Hindu and Buddhist Sanskrit literature. This anonymous Vajrapāṇi name-hymn belongs to the Newar Vajrayana liturgical repertoire and is preserved in Nepalese manuscripts digitized by the DSBC.

title: Mahābodhibhaṭṭārakastotra
author: anonymous/traditional
summary: A hymn honouring the Mahābodhi Bhaṭṭāraka, the 'Venerable Great Enlightenment' — the Buddha as enshrined at the Mahābodhi temple in Bodhgayā, the site of his awakening, often as a venerated image. It praises the Buddha's enlightenment and the sacred seat (vajrāsana) of his victory.
history: Bodhgayā remained a pilgrimage focus for Nepalese and other Buddhists, and replicas of the Mahābodhi temple/image were venerated in Nepal. This anonymous praise-poem is listed among Buddhist Sanskrit stotras and preserved through the Nepalese manuscript tradition and DSBC digitization.

title: Mahābodhivandanāṣṭaka
author: anonymous/traditional
genre: stotra (vandanā-aṣṭaka, salutation hymn of eight verses)
summary: An eight-verse hymn of salutation (vandanā) to the Mahābodhi — the Great Enlightenment of the Buddha and its sacred site at Bodhgayā. Each verse offers homage to the awakened Buddha and the place of his enlightenment.
history: An anonymous Buddhist Sanskrit aṣṭaka of homage to Bodhgayā/the Mahābodhi, part of the devotional literature surrounding the cult of the enlightenment site. Preserved in Nepalese Buddhist manuscripts and listed in the DSBC/Buddhist stotra corpus.

title: Māravijayastotra
author: anonymous/traditional
summary: A hymn celebrating the Buddha's 'Victory over Māra' (māravijaya) — his defeat of the tempter-demon Māra and his hosts beneath the Bodhi tree on the night of his enlightenment. The text praises the Buddha's serene conquest of fear, desire, and death.
history: The conquest of Māra is among the most celebrated episodes of the Buddha's life and a recurrent subject of Sanskrit Buddhist poetry. This anonymous stotra was transliterated and proofread for the Digital Sanskrit Buddhist Canon project and is preserved through the Nepalese manuscript tradition.

title: Vajrasattvastotra
author: anonymous/traditional
summary: A hymn in praise of Vajrasattva, the 'Diamond Being,' the primordial bodhisattva-buddha who embodies the purity of awakened mind and is central to Vajrayana purification practice. It extols his luminous, indestructible nature and his role in cleansing defilements.
history: Vajrasattva is a key figure of Vajrayana liturgy, especially purification rites. This anonymous praise-poem belongs to the Newar Buddhist stotra repertoire and is preserved in Nepalese manuscripts and the DSBC corpus.

title: Vajrasattvastuti
author: anonymous/traditional
summary: A praise-hymn (stuti) to Vajrasattva, the embodiment of pure awakened mind in Vajrayana Buddhism. A companion piece to the corresponding stotra, it glorifies the deity's purity and his power to remove transgressions and obscurations.
history: An anonymous Buddhist Tantric praise of Vajrasattva preserved in the Nepalese manuscript tradition and digitized within the DSBC/Sanskrit stotra corpus; author and date are unrecorded.

title: Kalyāṇatriṃśatikāstotra
author: anonymous/traditional
summary: A hymn of thirty verses (triṃśatikā) of auspicious praise (kalyāṇa) addressed to Trikāyanivāsinī Vajradevī, the 'Vajra Goddess who dwells in the three bodies' (trikāya) of the Buddha. It exalts the goddess as embodiment of the Buddha's body, speech, and mind.
history: Catalogued in the Sanskrit stotra corpus as 'kalyāṇatriṃśatikāstotram trikāyanivāsinīvajradevyāḥ,' linking it to the Vajradevī cult of Newar Vajrayana. It is an anonymous hymn preserved in Nepalese manuscripts with scans and text digitized by the DSBC and Sanskrit Documents.

title: Vajradevīstotra
author: anonymous/traditional
summary: A hymn praising Vajradevī, the 'Vajra Goddess' (associated with Trikāyanivāsinī Vajradevī), a Tantric female deity venerated in Newar Buddhism. The text extols her divine attributes and her embodiment of awakened power.
history: An anonymous Vajrayana stotra to Vajradevī, closely connected with the Kalyāṇatriṃśatikā hymn to Trikāyanivāsinī Vajradevī. Preserved in Nepalese Buddhist manuscripts and digitized within the DSBC/Sanskrit stotra corpus.

title: Vajravilāsinīsādhanāstava
author: anonymous/traditional
genre: stava/stotra embedded in or accompanying a sādhana
summary: A hymn of praise (stava) connected with the sādhana (meditative evocation ritual) of Vajravilāsinī, a Tantric form of the female buddha Vajrayoginī/Vajravārāhī in her 'playful, voluptuous' (vilāsinī) aspect. The text accompanies visualization practice, praising the deity as she is to be evoked.
history: Vajravilāsinī is a form of Vajrayoginī within the Anuttarayoga/Mother-Tantra cycle (related to Cakrasaṃvara and Hevajra). Such sādhana-linked stavas circulated in Newar Buddhist ritual; this anonymous text is preserved in Nepalese manuscripts and the DSBC corpus.

title: Vajravilāsinīstotra
author: anonymous/traditional
summary: A hymn in praise of Vajravilāsinī, a graceful, voluptuous form of the female buddha Vajrayoginī. It glorifies her iconography and her embodiment of blissful awakened wisdom in the Tantric tradition.
history: An anonymous Vajrayana praise of Vajravilāsinī (a Vajrayoginī/Vajravārāhī form), companion to the associated sādhana-stava. Preserved in the Nepalese Buddhist manuscript tradition and digitized within the DSBC/Sanskrit stotra corpus.

title: Vajrayoginīpraṇāmaikaviṃśikā
author: anonymous/traditional
genre: stotra (praṇāma, hymn of homage in twenty-one verses)
summary: A hymn of homage (praṇāma) in twenty-one verses (ekaviṃśatikā) to Vajrayoginī, the female buddha and ḍākinī of the Mother-Tantra class. Each verse offers prostration to an aspect or form of the deity, on the model of the well-known twenty-one praises to Tārā.
history: Vajrayoginī's cult developed in India between the 10th and 12th centuries out of the Cakrasaṃvara tradition and became central in Newar and Tibetan Vajrayana. This anonymous twenty-one-verse homage is preserved in Nepalese manuscripts and digitized in the DSBC corpus. (The original title is given as 'Vajrayoginīpraṇāmaikaviśikā,' read here as ...ekaviṃśikā.)

title: Vajrayoginyāḥ piṇḍārthastutiḥ
author: anonymous/traditional
summary: A 'praise of the essential meaning' (piṇḍārtha-stuti) of Vajrayoginī, condensing the core significance of the deity and her practice into a hymn of praise. It glorifies Vajrayoginī as the female buddha embodying the union of bliss and emptiness.
history: An anonymous Vajrayana hymn distilling the essence (piṇḍārtha) of the Vajrayoginī cult, part of the Newar Buddhist devotional repertoire and preserved in Nepalese manuscripts digitized by the DSBC.

title: Vajrayoginyāḥ stutipraṇidhānam
author: anonymous/traditional
genre: stotra combined with praṇidhāna (praise and aspiration prayer)
summary: A combined 'praise-and-aspiration' (stuti-praṇidhāna) directed to Vajrayoginī, joining a hymn of praise with a formal aspirational prayer (praṇidhāna) in which the devotee dedicates merit and vows to attain the deity's awakened state.
history: An anonymous Vajrayana liturgical text uniting praise with aspiration, characteristic of Mahayana/Vajrayana devotional structure. Preserved in the Nepalese Buddhist manuscript tradition and digitized in the DSBC corpus.

title: Vasudhārānāmadhāraṇīstotra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: transmitted in Nepal since at least the 11th c. CE; underlying Vasudhārā dhāraṇī is earlier (1st millennium CE)
summary: A hymn-cum-dhāraṇī devoted to Vasudhārā ('Stream of Gems'), the bodhisattva-goddess of wealth, prosperity, and abundance. Combining praise with the efficacious dhāraṇī formula, it is recited to secure material and spiritual prosperity, drawing on the Vasudhārā Dhāraṇī and the legend of the layman Sucandra.
history: Vasudhārā has been especially venerated in Newar Buddhism of the Kathmandu Valley since at least the 11th century (early Nepalese sculptures from 1082 CE), with her cult rooted in the Vasudhārā Dhāraṇī. This anonymous dhāraṇī-stotra belongs to that devotional tradition and is preserved in Nepalese manuscripts digitized by the DSBC.
